q: In a period, the valence shell electronic configuration of transition elements does not change.
Transition elements have the same group similarities as the $s$-block and $p$-block elements.
Transition elements are silvery-white except for copper, which is brownish-red in colour.
Transition elements are generally poor conductors of electricity and heat.
The transition elements have a high density, with iron being 8 times heavier than sodium per cubic centimetre.
Transition elements are significantly more reactive than Group I metals.
Nickel and copper do not react with water or catch fire in the air (oxygen).
In general, transition metals corrode readily in the atmosphere.
Iron combined with non-metal carbon produces an alloy called steel.
Most transition elements form coloured compounds, while Group I and II metals form white compounds.
Transition elements show variable oxidation states because they can only form ions with a $+2$ charge.
Most transition elements can form complex ions.
Compared to Alkali metals, $3d$ series transition metals have low reactivity and do not react vigorously with water.
Transition elements usually lose electrons from the $d$ orbital first, followed by the $s$ orbital.
The $+3$ oxidation states ($M^{3+}$ ions) are more stable at the beginning of the $3d$ series.
Towards the end of the $3d$ series, $+2$ oxidation states ($M^{2+}$ ions) are more stable.
The elements in the middle of the series exhibit fewer oxidation states than those to the left or right.
Iron ($_{26}Fe$) is known to form oxidation states from $+2$ to $+6$.
The highest oxidation state is $+7$ for manganese ($_{25}Mn$).
Ions with the highest oxidation states, like permanganate ($MnO_4^-$), tend to be good oxidising agents.
Vanadium and chromium ions with the lowest oxidation state of $+2$ are good reducing agents.
Divalent ions of $Co^{2+}$, $Ni^{2+}$, $Cu^{2+}$, and $Zn^{2+}$ are excellent reducing agents.
Transition metals usually have their highest oxidation states in compounds with oxygen and fluorine.
The colour of compounds of transition metals is due to the presence of incomplete $d$ orbitals or unpaired electrons.
Transition metal compounds form coloured ions because they transmit part of the visible light and absorb the complement colour.
$Sc^{3+}$ and $Zn^{2+}$ ions are colourless because $Sc^{3+}$ has completely empty $d$ orbitals and $Zn^{2+}$ has completely filled $d$ orbitals.
If the electrons in $(n-1)d$ orbitals are paired, then the substance is a paramagnetic substance.
If the electrons in $(n-1)d$ orbitals are unpaired, then it is a paramagnetic substance.
Copper and zinc are paramagnetic substances.
Scandium, titanium, vanadium, chromium, and manganese are paramagnetic substances.
Substances that are attracted very strongly by the applied magnetic field are said to be ferromagnetic.
Iron, cobalt, and nickel are ferromagnetic substances.
Transition metals are widely used at the industrial level due to their catalytic properties.
Iron is used as a catalyst in the Contact process.
Vanadium(V) oxide ($V_2O_5$) is used as a catalyst in the manufacture of sulphuric acid.
Nickel is used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes.
$Titanium(IV)$ chloride ($TiCl_4$) is used for the manufacturing of high-density polyethene.
A complex ion has a metal ion at its centre with a number of other molecules or ions surrounding it.
Paramagnetic substances show weak attraction towards the external magnetic field.
Diamagnetic substances show a strong attraction to the external magnetic field.
Ferromagnetic substances retain their magnetisation for some time even after the external magnetic field is removed.
